critique.summary: The article claims that Walmart's ChatGPT-based checkout system performed three times worse than its traditional website checkout process. However, the provided content does not include detailed information or evidence supporting this claim.
mainPoints: Walmart implemented a ChatGPT-based checkout system.
The ChatGPT checkout system reportedly had a significantly lower conversion rate.
The conversion rate was three times worse than the traditional website checkout.
critique.truthfulness: The provided content does not contain verifiable information or supporting evidence for the claims made about Walmart's ChatGPT checkout system. The page appears to be a security verification page from Cloudflare, not an article or report, making it impossible to assess the accuracy of the claims based solely on this data.
considerations: The article lacks direct references, data, or sources to substantiate the performance claims.
Without additional context or official statements from Walmart or independent analyses, the claims remain unverified.
The content provided is a security verification page, not an actual journalistic report.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Walmart's ChatGPT checkout feature underperforming compared to its website, with many commenters attributing this to fundamental challenges in e-commerce optimization, user intent, and technical limitations of AI. While some see potential in AI-assisted discovery and future improvements, most agree that current AI checkout solutions add friction and fail to match established, optimized shopping experiences.

comments.author: hownottowrite
comments.text:
I’ve been running e-commerce systems for 30 years (tech, marketing, etc). This was going to fail from the start for one reason: intent.

Most people using AI chat are exploring ideas and solutions. They’re doodling, not shopping. Or in old timey parlance, they’re looky-loos or tire kickers at best.

Anyone who’s had to justify ad spend in e-commerce can tell you that some sources produce huge traffic with absolutely terrible conversion. Reddit and Pinterest pretty much blow for this reason, with limited exceptions. It’s also why TikTok and other influencer platforms really work.

Conversion requires a mental shift from discovery to demand.

Also, really hate summaries like this without the actual source so here are the main points from the actual source (WIRED https://archive.is/7DuEV):

1. Instant Checkout inside ChatGPT performed poorly, with conversion about one-third of Walmart’s normal site.

2. The experience failed largely because it forced single-item purchases instead of letting users build a cart.

3. Walmart is shifting to embedding its own assistant, Sparky, inside ChatGPT and keeping checkout on its own system.

4. ChatGPT is still valuable because it’s driving significantly more new customer traffic than search.

5. Purchases that did work were mostly practical, problem-solving items like supplements and tools.

6. Fully automated “agentic shopping” is still unlikely in the near term because people want control over purchases.

7. OpenAI is moving away from in-chat checkout and focusing on helping users research while merchants handle transactions.

In short, AI is useful for discovery, but traditional e-commerce flows still outperform it at closing sales.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced understanding of user behavior and intent, explaining why AI chat users may not convert well in checkout compared to traditional e-commerce customers.

comments.author: janalsncm
comments.text:
This is from one of the links in the article

> Why this is happening. Two forces are slowing agentic commerce, according to Leigh McKenzie, director of online visibility at Semrush: infrastructure and trust. Real-time catalog normalization across tens of millions of SKUs is a decade-scale problem Google already solved with Merchant Center, and consumers still default to checkout flows they trust — Apple Pay, Google Wallet, and Amazon one-click.

It turns out when you step outside of “hard tech” problems like building GPT6 there are all of these details others have solved already. E-commerce has been optimized to the last decimal point for the last 30 years.

OpenAI is new to it, and if I had to guess, not that interested in getting good at it.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights real technical and trust barriers in AI commerce, referencing known industry challenges and established payment preferences.

comments.author: justacatbot
comments.text: The catalog normalization problem is real and severely underestimated. I ran into this building on top of product data feeds -- even with a single retailer, inconsistency in titles, attributes, and categorization is staggering. LLMs are great at reasoning but they inherit all that messy upstream data, so agentic shopping will keep stumbling until the data layer gets cleaner.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately identifies a key technical challenge in AI shopping related to messy product data, which impacts AI's ability to provide seamless commerce experiences.

comments.author: mosaibah
comments.text: Checkout conversion is the wrong metric. Walmart's funnel has 20 years of A/B tests behind every button placement, so of course a brand-new chat interface loses there. The interesting question is whether chat wins at discovery, not checkout
comments.analysis: This comment provides a valid perspective that discovery and checkout are distinct phases, and AI might be more suited to discovery than closing sales.

comments.author: runako
comments.text:
This partnership was doomed to fail.

Walmart does not, over 10 years after they were released, even accept the contactless payment systems in common use. Instead, they push their in-house version in part so they can capture the relevant customer data.

And we're meant to believe that Walmart planned to outsource the entire series of touchpoints represented by the discovery & checkout process? Yeah, okay.

This was never going to be more than an experiment for Walmart.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully points out Walmart's payment ecosystem limitations and strategic motives, explaining why the partnership was unlikely to succeed.

comments.author: bilekas
comments.text:
ChatGPT Checkout, a solution looking for a problem.

Why would anyone have an extra layer of friction too where things could go wrong, where handing over payment details in another chain.

Just let me buy my stuff in peace. Shopping is not the 'killer app' for GenAI.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly identifies the friction and trust issues involved in adding AI as an intermediary in payment processes.

comments.author: asimpletune
comments.text:
Isn’t there already a much older rule that predicts this?

Your product has to be a 10x improvement over the incumbent to be competitive.

In AI speak it would be the “extra-bitter” lesson I guess?

You need to add 10x resources to beat a product that’s already solved with mature tech.
comments.analysis: This comment references a well-known innovation rule, emphasizing the high bar AI must clear to disrupt established e-commerce systems.

comments.author: bgirard
comments.text:
I think they're thinking about this wrong.

I get all my groceries deliver to my doorstep via Walmart delivery pass. The thing I'm really missing is having AI curate meal planning to my family's preferences. I already feed ChatGPT my family' preferences (e.g. Kid A doesn't eat X Y Z and liked meal A B C, kid B likes ...) and ChatGPT is helping me build meal plans. With my preferences we can quickly nail down a meal plan for the week.

The slowest part of my meal planning is going through Walmart's slow site where each page load is 2-3 seconds and it takes several page load per item. Once it can translate my meal plan into a grocery checkout from Walmart I'm all set.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a constructive use case for AI in e-commerce, focusing on discovery and planning rather than checkout conversion.

comments.author: hexasquid
comments.text: Last year they couldn't draw fingers on hands properly, this year they can't convert at checkouts, I wonder what they'll be failing to do a year from now.
comments.analysis: This comment is more sarcastic and emotional, highlighting skepticism about AI progress but lacking constructive insight.

comments.author: keiferski
comments.text:
I don’t trust AI bots to access my wallet. Not sure I ever will.

I sort of trust them to make product recommendations, but at best I will only open a link they suggest and buy the product there.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects common consumer trust issues with AI handling payments, which is a valid concern but anecdotal.
